story:
My favourite tree stood by our driveway: a Jacaranda from South America. Jim and I nailed timber offcuts into its trunk for a ladder. He liked the highest branches; the first few were enough for me. Jim found rope on a fishing trip and made a swing to play jungles. Rope was always a precious commodity, useful and scarce.

One morning Patrick, my father, called us to the tree and whispered, “Look.” Two Boobook owls blinked from the first branch. “Quiet now,” he said, “they’re nocturnal.” That means they sleep during the day. I’m never sure that’s always true; I’ve seen plenty of “nocturnal” animals up and about in sunlight.

Near the chook pen grew a Loquat—Jim’s tree. He spent weekends up there, singing and munching loquats. He built a platform from dried Calistemon branches, lashing it with wool pinched from Mum’s sewing kit. I wasn’t sure it would hold. It did.

On bush walks with our dog Tobias, we passed old Jarrah scorched by fires, some with charcoal caves in their trunks. “Whisper trees,” Patrick said. “That’s where the animals have their secret meetings.” Jim loved that, and Patrick would wink so I wouldn’t spoil it.

We had Red Gums too. In high heat they bled sticky crimson sap onto the trampoline, which we dodged like poison. Their gum nuts made whistles if you capped them with your thumb and blew. Cockatoos swarmed, nibbling and dropping them so fast we invented a game called the gauntlet: bike helmets on, sprint under the crown and back, weaving through the plummeting projectiles.

By the Paperbark, Jim and I peeled a careful sheet and snuck Mum and Patrick’s best inky pen to draw a treasure map, an old X leading straight to the Jacaranda. I loved maps, especially drawing them.

Patrick was fond of adventures. “Into the city for ice cream,” he declared, with his customary wink. In the park, colossal Moreton Bay figs sprawled, their serpentine roots making hollows for hiding. They stank to high heaven and were the best trees for climbing. Still, the Jacaranda was my favourite. In hot months it dropped purple flowers. We scooped them into a bucket, and with our sisters Jane and Lizzie played weddings, tossing confetti. Purple was my colour; red was Jim’s. After the vows we scattered the petals on the trampoline and, on my count, bounced them in a purple frenzy.

Then one Tuesday after school we pulled in to the rumble of chainsaws. From the back seat, before the house came into view, Jim said, “They’ve poisoned it purple.” The space in the sky hit me in the chest. The men in yellow shirts coiled ropes and raked leaves. The tree was a stump. Patrick stood with his hands pocketed. “What happened?” I cried. Mum said the roots were lifting the driveway and the branches were pushing the telephone wire. A new drive and wire cost too much. Jim was speechless. I confronted Patrick. “Why didn’t you ask me?” He was solemn. “I’m sorry, Sunshine. It had to be done.” They even poisoned the stump purple.

After Mass that Sunday I found him on the roof, cricket on the wireless. “I’m upset,” I said, “but I need your help to make it right.” I showed him my plan, method and diagrams on fresh matrix paper. He smiled. “Very impressive.” “Can it be done?” “Let’s get to it, boss.” From the shed he emerged with an axe and bellowed, “Lay on, Macduff!” “And damned be him that first cries ‘Hold! Enough!’” I answered. He chopped only the stump’s centre, purple chips flying, then we filled the hollow with soil and sowed a Lilac vine.

Patrick was a good man. The stump is still there, the vine bursting out in vivid green and vibrant purple. I loved that tree. It’s still my favourite.
